entry.title: Re: Data Integration in ABAP Cloud: Data Exposure using SQL services
entry.content:
Hello @akuller 

Writing via SQL is mainly suitable for technical scenarios (imports, replication). Writing business logic via SQL would be architecturally possible, but RAP does not support it yet. Current recommendation is to write through business logic using OData.

Writable External Entities can execute Insert/Update/Delete operations in HANA databases. However RAP does not yet support this.  Besides, SQL Service cannot yet be used as a writable target due to missing SDA-level features. We shall wait for future releases to come!

Best regards

Safa

entry.title: How to add a check for requisitioner for purchase requisition created from plant maintenance orders?
entry.content:
Hi experts,

We have a requirement to add a validation for the requisitioner field before creating Purchase requisition(s) from a Plant maintenance order. The Purchase requisition(s) could be created immediately from operation lines or external component lines while creating and saving the Plant maintenance order.

Requirement:

  • Restrict the saving of the PM order and the creation of the Purchase requisition if the validation fails with an error.
  • Return to the order creation/ change screen (IW31/ IW32) in case of error (error shouldn't terminate current session)
  • Allow the data in the PM order to be edited (error shouldn't block editing)
  • We need an enhancement that has all the necessary inputs for operation lines and components and allow to restrict the commit for saving the PM order and creation of the purchase requisition

What we have checked so far:

  • I tried to check the WORKORDER_UPDATE BAdI using the method AT_SAVE, but this method lacks the information on the current operation lines and the components as inputs.
  • Then we tried adding the validating in BEFORE_UPDATE method of WORKORDER_UPDATE BAdI, but this method gets called after the AT_SAVE method, so it doesn't serve the purpose.

If anyone has any experience with handling such validation, please share your inputs. Thanks in advance!

Regards,
Aavil

entry.title: Re: BOM Status with Change Number
entry.content:
Hello @nuno_pedroso1

Based on the screenshots, the production version itself appears to be maintained correctly. The consistency checks shows that both the task list exists and the BOM exists and the production version is valid from 19.02.2026. Also, your BOM with change number is valid from 02.02.2026, so from a pure master data perspective the setup looks consistent.

What I see in your screenshots is that you created the order with Scheduling Type 4 Current date. In standard SAP scheduling, this means the system uses the current date as the order start date. If the current date is earlier than 19.02.2026, the system may still reject the production version and issue C2142, even though the version appears in the selection list.

I recommend you create the production order with an order date that is explicitly on or after 19.02.2026 and then test the same production version again. If that works, then the issue is caused by the order key date rather than by Status 3 itself. If it still fails, then the setup shown in your screenshots would justify raising an SAP incident, because the BOM, task list and production version seem to appear all correct.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Prepare for refresh with /SDF/CALM_SYS_REFRESH
entry.content:
Good day All

We are planning a refresh of a DEV system and I need to export(backup) the Cloud ALM tables from the DEV system with report /SDF/CALM_SYS_REFRESH.

The report is mentioned in the house keeping section of below link

SAP S/4HANA and SAP Business Suite 7 | SAP Help Portal

 

 

Doing the export with the report is not an issue but I am struggling to understand how to manage the transport of copies that the report creates.

The export must be done for the DEV system and the transport will obviously not be imported to QA and PRD as the tables are system specific. The report creates a backup of the tables which should be imported back into the DEV system after the refresh.

The transport of copies is created without a target system. When I try to release the transport I get a warning that I need to Specify a transport target.

If I edit the transport of copies to add a Target I add the DEV system details and get an error: Source client = target client not possible for transports.

I can still Save the Target details even though the error is displayed, but when I try to release the Transport of Copies, I get similar error as above.

I am not familiar with transport of copies and I guess when I release the ToC it shoud create the data and co files for the transport, which should be used to reimport the tables into the DEV system after the refresh.

Is there another way that the ToC should be released, something else that should be added as the Target or does "Export this request and save it" means something else?

Appreciate any guidance.

Werner

entry.title: Re: Transferring Cost Centers in a Service Organization
entry.content:
Hello @db11 

Thank you for your question.

In Public Cloud, the Profession Services projects is designed that a service organisation is linked to a central cost center, and once you have created a project, the project’s service organisation cannot be changed. Because of this, if you remove the cost center from the original service organisation while the project is still ongoing, the project can fail validation when you try to save changes, since the project still references that original service organisation.

Therefore, the standard approach for an organisational change is usually not to move the cost center away from the original service organisation for active projects. Instead, you should keep the old service organisation and its cost center assignment for the ongoing projects and create or use a new service organisation with its own cost center for newly created projects going forward. Doing this will help you avoid inconsistencies and align with SAP’s restriction that the service organisation of an existing project cannot be changed after you have created the project.

See the following reference for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/29cf986299714386847f4d4c9bb86994/f487a450daa94f5da93fb7049a125184.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/29cf986299714386847f4d4c9bb86994/ccd0da55c2c4727fe10000000a44538d.html?locale=en-US

I recommend you:

  • Check your ongoing project in the Professional Services Projects app
  • Keep your current service organisation to cost center assignment as long as the projects remains active
  • Create a new service organisation in Manage Service Organisations
  • Thereafter, assign the new cost center there and use the new service organisation only for new projects after the re-org date.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: End-to-End Mail Adapter Setup in SAP CPI Using Gmail (Step-by-Step Guide)
entry.content:
Introduction :  This article provides a comprehensive end-to-end guide to configuring the Mail Receiver Adapter in SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) using a personal Gmail account. It explains the complete setup process, including the required Gmail configuration, security settings, and the detailed steps to configure the Mail Receiver Adapter within an integration flow. 

  • Enabling 2-Step Verification in Gmail

  • Generating a secure App Password

  • Creating User Credentials in CPI Security Material

Step 1 : Setting up the app password in Gmail.

To set up the app password 

  • Log in to your Gmail account.

  • Go to Google Account → Security.

  • Enable 2-Step Verification (mandatory).

  • After enabling 2-Step Verification, you will see App passwords.

  • Create a new App Password:

    • App: Mail

    • Device: Other (Custom name – SAP CPI)

  • Google will generate a 16-character password.

  • Use this password in CPI (without spaces).

Note : App Password works only if 2-Step Verification is enabled.

After creation you'll get a password which is generated 

Step 2 : Now we have  to store these credentials in cpi security material.

  • Go to Monitor → Manage Security → Security Material

  • Create User Credentials

  • Name: (e.g., gmailcredentials)

  • Username: Full Gmail address

  • Password: App Password (not Gmail login password)

The name which we have given here will be used in within the I flow

And the user should be  our mail ID  and in password we have give the app password which is generated.

Step 3 : Connectivity Test (certificate upload)

Now we have to test the connectivity towards the Gmail in Test connectivity tile.

  • Go to Monitor → Integrations & APIs → Connectivity Tests

  • Choose SMTP

  • Provide:

    • Host: smtp.gmail.com

    • Port: 587

    • Protection: STARTTLS

    • Authentication: Plain User/Password

    • Credential Name: gmailcredentials

Keystore: Upload the downloaded certificates here.

Step 4 : Create Integration Flow.

Here I have taken a simple content modifier with a body so that the message in content modifier will be triggered to our inbox

The body : 

 

Mail adapter : The To address should be same as what we have deployed in security material user credentials.

Step 5 : Testing 

In postman give the credential details and test it you'll getting the response and the mail will be triggered to your Gmail as well

We'll get a mail as below.

Summary : This blog explains how to configure and connect the SAP CPI Mail Adapter with a personal Gmail account. It covers the necessary Gmail security setup, including enabling 2-Step Verification and generating an App Password for secure authentication. The guide also walks through the step-by-step configuration of the Mail Receiver Adapter in SAP Cloud Platform Integration (CPI) and demonstrates how to send emails successfully using the  Gmail account configured.

entry.title: Attachment of Supporting Documents to Purchase Requisition (PR) – Item Level using DMS (CV01N)
entry.content:
The purpose of this document is to describe the process of attaching supporting documents (PDF/Word/Excel) to a Purchase Requisition (PR) at Item Level using SAP Document Management System (DMS).

This ensures:

  • Proper documentation for audit and compliance

  • Availability of technical specifications for approvers

  • Version control and traceability

  • Centralized document storage

Scope

This process applies to:

  • Purchase Requisitions with Item Level Release Strategy.

  • Documents such as technical specifications, quotations, drawings, approvals

  • Users with DMS authorization

Transaction Code Description

CV01NCreate Document Info Record
ME53NDisplay Purchase Requisition
ME52NChange Purchase Requisition

Prerequisites

  • PR must be created.

  • User must have DMS authorization.

  • Document Type for PR must be configured.

  • If required, PR item should be released before attachment.

    Procedure

    Step 1: Create Document Info Record

     

  1. Go to transaction CV01N.

  2. Enter:

    • Document Type (e.g., ZPR / DOC – as per configuration)

    • Description

    • Document Status

  3. Press Enter.

Note:

Document Status controls editability and release of the document.

Step 2: Maintain Object Links (Link PR Item)

  1. Go to Object Links tab.

  2. Enter:

    • Object Type → BANF

    • PR Number

    • PR Item Number

  3. Confirm the entries.

This links the document specifically to the PR Item.

 

Step 3: Upload Original File

  1. Go to Originals tab.

  2. Click Create.

  3. Select document type (PDF/Word/Excel).

  4. Enter description.

  5. Click on Original option.

  6. Select file from local system.

  7. Click ✔ (Tick).

After confirmation, the file appears in the Originals list

 

8. After, you are able to see the word document in the list of original tab. 

Step 4: Save Document

  1. Click Save.

  2. System generates Document Number (if internal numbering is configured).

  3. Document is now successfully linked to PR Item.

      4. Then Go to me53n t-code. 

Step 5: Verify Attachment in PR

  1. Go to transaction ME53N.

  2. Enter PR number.

  3. Select the relevant line item.

  4. Click on Document Button in item overview.

  5. Select document and click Display.

The attached file (PDF/Word) opens successfully.

 

Output:

Technical Information

     Object Type

  • PR Business Object: BUS2105

  • Object Link Type: BANF

    Important Tables

    Table Description
    DRAWDocument Header
    DRADObject Link
    DRAOOriginal Files
    SRGBTBRELAttachment relationships

    Workflow Relevance

    If PR release workflow is active:

    • Approvers can view attached DMS documents.

    • Attachments are accessible during release approval.

    • If document is not visible, check workflow container binding.

      Common Issues and Troubleshooting

      Issue Possible Cause Resolution
      Document not visible in ME53NObject link missingCheck DRAD table
      File not openingContent server issueCheck content repository configuration
      No authorizationMissing DMS rolesCheck SU53
      Document not linked to itemIncorrect item number enteredRecheck Object Link

      Advantages of Using DMS (CV01N)

      • Version Control

      • Centralized Document Storage

      • Change Management Support

      • Audit Compliance

      • Engineering Drawing Management

      • Classification Capability.

        Conclusion

        By using CV01N to attach documents at PR Item Level:

        • Documents are securely stored in DMS.

        • Linked properly using Object Type BANF.

        • Accessible during PR display and workflow approval.

        • Ensures compliance and document traceability.

entry.title: Re: Replicate the QR Code to custom Smartform, decoding f...
entry.content:
In a smartform you have two options for a QR code:

  1. system font: you pass the string value to the text node, set the appropriate paragraph / character type and done. Main problem: sizing and positioning can be extremely finicky
  2. embedded image: you can generate an image dynamically, you can even come up with a dynamic name and pass it to the graphic node but you cannot pass the image content itself to the graphic node! This works in Adobe Forms, not in SF.

What I did for a customer is: generate the image in the print program, save it to a dynamically-named image (so that concurrent printing of different invoices is possible), in the smartform read the appropriate name of the picture, when form is done remove the picture from DB.

 

*

entry.title: Implementing Bulk Upsert using Snowflake Adapter in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Overview 

In many integration projects, loading data into Snowflake is a common requirement. You may have used different approaches to insert or upsert data into Snowflake tables using Snowflake adapter depending on the system landscape and architecture : 

  • Insert operation in the adapter 
  • Executing SQL queries (Multi statements) 
  • Bulk Upsert Using External Stage (AWS S3, Azure Blob, or GCS) 

All these approaches work well. However, each may present itself with certain considerations.

For example: 

  • The Insert operation requires metadata configuration and may not be ideal for large datasets because constructing full INSERT statements can be overkilling while processing large datasets 
  • Executing queries means writing and managing SQL multi statements logic manually. 
  • External Stage requires hyperscaler storage (AWS, Azure, or GCS). 

Now let us assume a practical situation if the customer’s landscape does not include AWS, Azure, or GCS ?  

The latest version (1.2.0) of the adapter now also supports loading bulk data through  Snowflake Internal Stage. This is suggested for scenarios where user doesn’t have third-party storage services.

What’s New in the Snowflake Adapter?

The Snowflake Adapter now supports: 

  • File Staging operation (Put, List, Get) 
  • Bulk Upsert and Unload Operation with Access Type: Internal Stage 

With the above enhancements, you can: 

  • Upload, List and Fetch a file directly from Snowflake. 
  • Use that staged file to perform a bulk upsert into a target table. 
  • Unload the data from the Snowflake table to a stage location.

Let us first understand two important concepts before diving into the demo. 

What is File Staging in Snowflake? 

File staging in Snowflake is the process of uploading structured files (such as CSV or JSON) to a temporary storage location called a stage, from where Snowflake can efficiently load or merge the data into tables. 

Instead of inserting records one by one, data is processed in bulk using staged files, making it faster, scalable, and more suitable for large data loads. 

What is Snowflake Internal Stage? 

A Snowflake Internal Stage is a storage location managed entirely within Snowflake where files can be temporarily stored before being loaded into tables. 

It does not require AWS, Azure, or GCP storage and allows you to upload and process files directly inside Snowflake, making bulk data loading simpler and more streamlined. 

Integration Scenario : Bulk Upsert from Internal Stage  

Imagine a business scenario where a large volume of transactional records from an ERP system needs to be ingested into Snowflake for centralized storage, reporting, and analytics. 

  •  A straightforward approach would be to fetch the data from the ERP system and push it directly into Snowflake. However, when dealing with thousands or even lakhs of records, this method can take considerable time. 
  • Another option would be to write and execute custom SQL queries (such as Multi statements) to handle bulk processing. But maintaining large queries for high volumes of data is time-consuming and increases the risk of errors. 
  • Additionally, using an External Stage requires AWS, Azure, or GCP storage in the landscape. If the customer does not have a hyperscaler environment, that approach is simply not feasible. This is where the Internal Stage feature in the Snowflake Adapter becomes highly relevant.

In this blog, I will walk you through how to bulk insert records into Snowflake using the Snowflake Adapter in SAP Integration Suite. We will also briefly look at how the execution is handled internally within Snowflake for better understanding. 

Prerequisites

Before we begin, let us assume that: 

  • A target table with the required schema already exists in Snowflake. 
  • The connection between Snowflake and SAP Integration Suite is properly configured and working. 
  • Internal Stage is created in Snowflake. 

Let us now deep dive into the end-to-end implementation. 

Iflow Design in SAP CI 

For this example we have used a CSV file with 25k records in the Content Modifier which is uploaded to snowflake stage in the next step and then used for bulk upsert.

File Staging using Snowflake Adapter

The file is uploaded to the Snowflake Internal Stage using the PUT command. During configuration of the adapter, both the file name and the name of the Internal Stage must be specified. If required, a specific folder path within the stage can also be provided. Otherwise, the file will be uploaded to the default location of the Internal Stage. 
 

Bulk Upsert Using Snowflake Adapter

In this step, the data is upserted into the Snowflake table using the file that was previously staged in the Internal Stage. The configuration requires specifying the target table name and the Internal Stage name from which the data will be read. The file name should also be specified, as it determines which file’s data will be loaded into the table. If no file name is provided, the adapter will process all files available in the directory. Additionally, if the file is located within a specific subdirectory inside the stage, the corresponding path should be mentioned as well 
 
1. Also, note that Stages in Snowflake have a defined file format that determines which type of files can be uploaded and how those files are processed. Therefore, if you want to process file formats that are different from the one defined in the stage, you must provide the necessary parameters explicitly. 

For example, in the optional parameters field, you can specify: 

FILE_FORMAT = (TYPE = CSV) FORCE = TRUE ON_ERROR = CONTINUE

 2. Additionally, the File Name field can contain multiple files. For example: 

'BulkUpsert_Hundred_Rows1.csv','BulkUpsert_Hundred_Rows2.csv'

 3. Another important point to highlight is that if the file stored in the internal stage is compressed, the bulk upsert operation can automatically decompress the file and then load the data into the Snowflake table. 

For more details on optional parameters, please refer to the official Snowflake documentation: 
Optional parameters in Snowflake 

Now the iflow designing and configuration is complete, the iflow is ready to deploy. 

You can verify the same in the Snowflake application.
 
File is being staged in internal stage.  

 

File picked from stage and data upserted in snowflake table (EMP_DATA).  

Final Thoughts 

Using Internal Stage with the Snowflake Adapter makes bulk data loading into Snowflake simpler and more efficient, especially when external cloud storage is not part of the system landscape.

In our upcoming blog, we will also explore how to extract data from Snowflake tables via Snowflake Adapter. Stay tuned!

entry.title: SAP Asset Performance Management Meets SAP Situation Handling - Extended Framework
entry.content:
In this blog, I will outline how the SAP Situation Handling Extended Framework can be leveraged to notify Master Data Specialist when Maintenance Planner creates a maintenance objects such as Task Lists, Maintenance Plans, while implementing SAP Asset Performance Management Recommendations.

Business Value:

  • Operational Efficiency - Automating notifications eliminates manual follow-ups and reduces delays in master data validation.
  • Improved Collaboration and Transparency - This integration reduces communication gaps and ensures that master data is aligned with operational needs.
  • Optimized maintenance strategies extend asset life cycles and lower total cost of ownership.

If you want to gain a deep understanding of SAP Situation Handling - Standard Framework and SAP Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management, dive into my latest blog posts mentioned below.

  • S/4HANA Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management (ISLM) and Situation Handling Scenarios: Part-1 
  • S/4HANA Intelligent Scenario Lifecycle Management (ISLM) and Situation Handling Scenarios: Part-2  

let us have a look at the high level process flow of how SAP APM Recommendations integrate with SAP Situation Handling Extended Framework.

SAP APM Recommendations:

SAP Situation Handling Extended Framework:

Also, If you want to gain knowledge on SAP Signavio Process Manager & Process Collaboration Hub, please check out my blog post mentioned below. This blog provides a broad outline of process modelling.

  • The nuts and bolts of a well-thought-out SAP Signavio : Process Manager & Process Collaboration Hub 

My Business Use Case:

In my organization, maintenance planners operationalize the SAP APM Recommendations through maintenance task list and maintenance plans. The master data specialists team ensures governance by monitoring these processes through automated notifications enabled by Situation Handling Extended Framework.

Let us first focus on the key concepts of "Situation Handling - Extended Framework" and setup a situation type-extended for a maintenance object: Task List.

Situation Object: Technical model of a business object used in situation handling. This includes CDS views, navigation targets, events, and actions.

Situation Scenario: Business scenario that builds on situation objects. There are two kinds of situation scenarios. 

  • Object-based: Business scenario that builds on situation objects such as a Task List or a Maintenance Plan or a Maintenance Item.
  • Message-based: Business scenario that builds on system messages  from run types and reports.

As per my business use case, we will be exploring object-based situation scenarios listed below.

Situation Template: Template serves as a blueprint for creating a situation type.

Situation Type: Description of a business situation. For example, how a situation instance is triggered and who is informed about it.

Situation Instance: Instance of an exceptional issue which is displayed to the end user. It is generated according to the settings of the enabled situation type:

  • For Object-based situations: Whenever the conditions defined for the situation type are fulfilled.
  • For Message-based situations: Based on the run type settings for scheduled application jobs.

When situation instances occur, specified end users see the situations in the "My Situations-Extended" app, and also receive notifications on SAP FLP, Emails.

In order to get started with the Situation Handling Extended Framework, we must first define "Responsibility Management" for our object-based situation scenarios.

Intelligent systems need to determine agents(Responsible Persons) who are automatically notified about events and who are responsible for business processes and objects. 

The predefined settings in the situation template determine that the notification addresses only members of the "MAINT_MGMT" team category.

Team Information:

Responsibility Definitions and Member Function options allow us to select further filters  to determine the users responsible. For Instance, let us choose Main Work Center, Maintenance Planner Group, Maintenance Planning Plant, and Maintenance Plant.

Team Owner and Team Members:

Once we have enabled the Responsibility Team, we can proceed for  configuring the situation types-extended.

 

SAP provides the templates as listed below.

For demonstration purpose, I have used the EAM_MAINTENANCE_TASK_LIST template and created a Situation Type-Extended with an ID : Z_CAL_MAINT_TASK_LIST - Calcination Maintenance Task List.

we can provide the header information and situation instances criteria. However, the navigational actions are predefined with respect to each situation type.

The navigation actions are navigation options to business apps that can help users to solve a situation. In our business use case, we have "Find Maintenance Task List" app as a navigation action.

Now, let us define the conditions,

Mini Note: When we create a Task List via SAP APM Recommendation as part of Implement Recommendations, system creates a task list with status "1-Created" and assigns respective planning plant. So, let us consider the fields that SAP APM considers while creating a Task List.

and Batch job Scheduling parameters.

A situation Instance is created when the overall status of a maintenance task list is "Created"  and situation/notification is displayed as per the predefined text template.

Now let us assign the team that was created in the earlier step.

Now let us enable the Situation Type.

Now, let us perform FMEA(Failure Mode and Effects Analysis) assessment and create a "Task List" for one of the FMEA recommendations.

For Instance, I have performed an FMEA on Equipment 10001730 (Reactor 101 DIN2816 AIE). Based on the resulting RPN score, a recommendation was created/reviewed/released for Implementation.

FMEA Assessment:

Creating a Recommendation:

Let us now use "Manage Recommendations" app to update, review, and release the recommendation.

Review:

Ready for Release:

Released:

Once the recommendations are released, maintenance planners can initiate the implementation process.

Implement Recommendations:

As part of the recommendation implementation process, maintenance planners have to update the status of the recommendation to "Implementation In Process".

Once the status becomes "Implementation In Process", the maintenance planner can either assign the existing task list and maintenance plan or create a new task list and maintenance plan.

Since there is no task list exists in S/4HANA for this recommendation, let us create a new task list for this recommendation.

Now, as per the conditions maintained in the Situation Type "Z_CAL_MAINT_TASK_LIST", the system triggered the notifications.

Monitor Situations - Extended app helps us monitor situations created by the extended framework for situation handling. Monitoring Situation instances is enabled by default. We can display the activities performed for situation instances using different chart types and also as a table. To capture and show monitoring data, we must need to enable "Monitor Instances" while configuring the situation type-extended.

Let us first check the Monitor Situations - Extended app to visualize the situation instances that have been created so far.

Now, let us view the Notifications.

Option-1: We can view the notifications using "Bell Icon" on FLP screen.

Options-2: We can view notifications using "My Situations-Extended" app. Using this app, we can view all the situations as per the responsibility area. 

 

Scenario Selection Area: The list view shows all situations. We can go to situation page for further information or close the situation directly in the list view.

Situation Page: The situation page displays the detail information of a situation as of the point in time it was created, however, this can differ from the current date.

The option "Assign To Me" : We can assign a situation to ourselves. Once we assign to ourselves, then the system updates processor details with our name. If we stop working on this situation, we can unassign ourselves and the respective situation is displayed as "Open".

Now let us use the "Find Maintenance Task List" app to update the task list operations.

"Find Maintenance Task List" app will navigate us to the "Change Task List" app.

Let us now update the required operations for the task list group 36 and counter 1.

Once the respective Task List is updated, we can close the situation.

😊This brings me to the end of my process walk through on how we can leverage SAP Situation Handling Extended Framework for SAP Asset Performance Management (APM) - Implement Recommendations. I hope this blog proves useful to the Maintenance Management Team. I look forward to the adoption of the Situation Handling Extended Framework as part of SAP APM implementation to improve operational efficiency.

 

--

Yours Sincerely,

Pardhasaradhi Reddy. Chelikam

entry.title: How can we export SAP CPQ custom tables with more than a million records?
entry.content:
In SAP CPQ, the Custom Table Export functionality allows exporting table entries to Excel.

However, Excel worksheets have a maximum limit of 1,048,576 rows.

What is the recommended or officially supported approach for exporting custom tables that contain more than one million records?


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How can we export SAP CPQ custom tables with more tha...
entry.content:
Excel files have a maximum limit of 1,048,576 rows per worksheet (Microsoft Excel limitation). Therefore, exporting more records than this limit into a single Excel file is not supported.

If a custom table contains more entries than this limit, the recommended approach is to export the data in batches by applying filters (for example, by ID ranges or other column filters) so that each export remains within Excel’s row limit. The exported files can then be combined if needed.

Alternatively, the ZIP export option may also be used, as it can be more suitable when exporting larger datasets.

entry.title: Data Integration in ABAP Cloud: Data Consumption using External Entities
entry.content:
In the previous post, we discussed data exposure using SQL services. In this post, we shift the focus to the data consumption scenario.

Imagine an ABAP Cloud system that needs to access data located elsewhere, perhaps in an SAP HANA Cloud database, or even in another ABAP system. With SQL Services, an ABAP system can itself act as a database. To support such outbound data access in a clean and cloud-compliant way, ABAP Cloud introduces a new CDS modelling artifact: CDS External Entities.

 

What Are CDS External Entities?

CDS External Entities enable outbound SQL access from an ABAP Cloud system. This means you can:

  • Access data from a remote database
  • Load and process remote data in ABAP
  • Combine remote data with local data

In ABAP Cloud, classic secondary database connections are not available. CDS External Entities are their more powerful and cloud-ready replacement.

Conceptually, a CDS External Entity is comparable to an SAP HANA virtual table; it represents a local view of a remote table or view. The remote object and its fields may have different names, which can be mapped explicitly.

CDS External Entities can be consumed in two different ways:

- Dynamic Access

In the SAP BTP ABAP environment, a CDS External Entity can be used directly in ABAP SQL. You write a SELECT statement that accesses data from a remote database, loads it, and processes it in ABAP.

- Static Access

Starting with release 2508, CDS view entities and classic CDS views can be built on top of external entities. This allows you to integrate remote data seamlessly into your CDS view modelling and treat it like local data. This feature will be used in my next blog post of the series.

Logical External Schema

Alongside CDS External Entities, there is a second important artifact: the Logical External Schema which is based on SAP HANA Smart Data Access (SDA). The key features are as follows:

  • It represents a remote system at design time.
  • It is mapped to a concrete remote source at configuration time.
  • It contains connection-related information (host, port, user, etc.).

From a runtime perspective, the ABAP system sends the query to its own HANA database, which then federates the query to the remote system using SDA.

 

How External Entities Work at Runtime

External entities allow remote data access using a new ABAP SQL syntax:

SELECT FROM <external_entity>

  PROVIDED BY <logical_external_schema>

  INTO TABLE @DATA(result).

In this flow:

  1. The ABAP application sends the SQL query to the local HANA database.
  2. HANA performs query federation using Smart Data Access.
  3. Data is retrieved from the remote database.
  4. Results are returned to the ABAP application server.

The <logical_external_schema> is a design-time artifact that points to the remote system and is bound to an actual remote source during configuration.

 

Example Scenario

Let us build an example based on the travel scenario in which

  • SAP HANA Cloud acts as the remote database.
  • The ABAP system consumes data via Dynamic CDS External Entities.

It is crucial that

  • The external entity has the same name and field structure as the remote HANA table.
  • Field names and types must match exactly (HANA is case-sensitive).

Besides, to enable the access, an outbound service and a communication scenario are required.

Design Time Steps

1. Define a Dynamic CDS External Entity

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #CHECK
@EndUserText.label: 'external entity for travel'
define external entity ZI_EE_TRAVEL_000 external name TRAVEL
{
  key TravelID      : abap.numc(8)                external name "TravelID";
      AgencyID      : abap.numc(6)                external name AgencyID;
      CustomerID    : abap.numc(6)                external name CustomerID;
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
      BookingFee    : abap.curr(16,2)             external name BookingFee;
      @Semantics.amount.currencyCode: 'CurrencyCode'
      TotalPrice    : abap.curr(16,2)             external name TotalPrice;
      CurrencyCode  : abap.cuky                   external name CurrencyCode;
      BeginDate     : abap.dats                   external name BeginDate;
      EndDate       : abap.dats                   external name EndDate;
      Description   : abap.sstring(1024)          external name Description;
      OverallStatus : abap.char(1)                external name OverallStatus;
      CreatedBy     : abp_creation_user           external name CreatedBy;
      CreatedAt     : abp_creation_tstmpl         external name CreatedAt;
      LastChangedBy : abp_locinst_lastchange_user external name LastChangedBy;
}
with federated data 
provided at runtime;

This entity models the structure of the remote table or view.

2. Define a Logical External Schema

This acts as a placeholder for the remote database and is referenced by the external entity.

3. Create Outbound Service and Communication Scenario

  • Create an SQL type outbound service for the logical external schema
  • Create a communication scenario and assign the outbound service to it. Then save and publish the scenario locally.

This enables administrators to configure access to the remote system

4. Consume the External Entity in ABAP SQL

Create a class and write ABAP SQL code

Select * from my external entity provided by my remote into table my local table

CLASS zcl_travel_consume_000 DEFINITION
  PUBLIC
  FINAL
  CREATE PUBLIC .

  PUBLIC SECTION.
    INTERFACES if_oo_adt_classrun .
  PROTECTED SECTION.
  PRIVATE SECTION.
ENDCLASS.

CLASS zcl_travel_consume_000 IMPLEMENTATION.
METHOD if_oo_adt_classrun~main.
     data: travel type table of zi_ee_travel_000.

    select * from zi_ee_travel_000 provided by z_travel_remote_db_000 into table @Travel.

    out->write( travel ).
    endmethod.

ENDCLASS.

In summary, what happens at runtime is as follows

  • The ABAP system sends the query to its HANA database
  • HANA SDA forwards the query to the remote database
  • Data is fetched and returned to the ABAP application
  • The local internal table is filled with remote data

Now we need to establish the connection between our development system and the SAP HANA Cloud database.

 

Configuration Time Steps

1. Create Communication Arrangement

In the SAP Fiori Launchpad:

  • Create a communication arrangement for the communication scenario you published in design time.

2. Maintain a communication system for the remote database

  • Activate Remote SQL-Access
  • Choose HANA ODBC as the SDA adapter
  • Provide host, port, driver, and additional connection settings
    (These details are typically provided by the system administrator)

save the communication system.

3. Maintain Remote Schema Information

In the communication arrangement:

  • Specify the schema name of the remote HANA database
  • Save the configuration and check whether the connection is successful

4. Prepare the Remote HANA Database

In Visual Studio Code and using SAP HANA Database Explorer:

  • Create a table that matches the CDS External Entity exactly
  • Ensure field names, types, and sizes match as well

Remember that SAP HANA is case-sensitive.

What Happens Behind the Scenes

When you execute the ABAP SQL statement:

  • The ABAP SQL layer resolves the external entity and logical external schema
  • HANA automatically:
    • Creates a remote source
    • Creates the required virtual table
  • Query federation is handled transparently

In this sense, CDS External Entities act as a smart abstraction that automatically manages HANA remote sources and virtual tables via ABAP design-time objects.

Press F9, and the data is returned as expected.

In the next blog post I would like to build a RAP application in the cloud, based on data I read from an on-premises system. So, stay tuned!

entry.title: 🇩🇪 SAP Fiori elements flexible programming model (Vienna, Austria)
entry.content:
This SAP CodeJam event is about the SAP Fiori elements flexible programming model (FPM). After a quick introduction to UI5, we will dive into the SAP Fiori elements and learn how to build a web application that allows users to browse and order books. Using the FPM, we will customise this SAP Fiori elements application. With this hands-on experience, you will be able to start developing your own SAP Fiori elements applications after this CodeJam.

The content for this SAP CodeJam is available on GitHub: https://github.com/SAP-samples/fiori-elements-fpm-exercises-codejam

This is an in-person event only (not virtual) and is planned for Friday 24 April 2026 from 09:00 - 15:00 local time. We will meet in the SAP Österreich office (first floor). The language of the event and the content will be English.

The instructor will be Developer Advocate Nico Schoenteich.

Please note that spaces are limited - we can only accommodate 30 attendees. Register here in the Community Event using the RSVP functionality.

For more information on SAP CodeJam events, to ask questions, and to chat with your fellow attendees, head over to the SAP CodeJam group.

entry.title: Now That You Have Talent Intelligence Hub, How to Maximize It for Workforce & Succession Planning
entry.content:
Implementing Talent Intelligence Hub (TIH) is a major milestone—but the real value comes from how effectively you use it. When fully enabled and adopted, TIH becomes a powerful engine for workforce planning, succession planning, and internal mobility, helping organizations move from reactive talent decisions to proactive, data-driven strategies.

Kudos to @RinkyKarthik  for the incredible work she is doing with the Talent Alchemy podcast and for the SAP SuccessFactors Talent Ecosystem!  A recent series on the show sparked a critical question: How can you maximize TIH for workforce or succession planning?  It’s a timely discussion that aligns perfectly with the insights in this blog post.

Below are key ways to maximize TIH and turn talent data into meaningful workforce insights.

 

  1. Enable and Optimize Talent Search

One of the fastest ways to unlock value from TIH is by ensuring Talent Search is enabled and properly permissioned.  Included in the Succession module, Talent Search is a powerful tool to assist in locating internal talent. 

 

  • Confirm that the right users (HR, talent partners, leaders) have access.
  • Make sure Skills are included in the search criteria under Ratings & Attributes.

Why it matters:
This allows you to quickly identify internal talent based on actual capabilities, not just job titles or resumes. For workforce planning, this means you can spot skill clusters, gaps, and ready-now talent across the organization.

 

  1. Use the Role Readiness Indicator for Successor Selection

Also within the Succession module, the ability to nominate successors to a position is fundamental for talent planning.  When selecting successors, enable the Role Readiness Indicator.

 

  • This displays a candidate’s readiness directly on the position nomination card.
  • The readiness indicator pulls from CDP (Career Development Planning) data.

Why it matters:
Leaders no longer have to guess whether someone is “ready now” or “ready later.” This creates more objective, transparent succession discussions and helps prioritize development actions for high-potential employees.

 

  1. Permission “View Role Details” in the Succession Org Chart

One final piece within the Succession module is to ensure users can access View Role Details directly from the Succession Org Chart.  The role detail view links to the job profile, also referred to as job description. 

 

  • This connects the role to the job profile from the Succession Org Chart (SOC).
  • Leaders can see role expectations, skills, and role requirements in context while actively planning when it’s needed most.

Why it matters:
Succession planning becomes role-centric, not person-centric. Leaders can align successors to what the role truly requires, improving bench strength and reducing risk when planning for critical positions.

 

  1. Turn On Suggested Roles in the Career Worksheet

The Suggested Roles functionality on the Career Worksheet enables employees to proactively seek new roles.  Suggested roles can use TIH associated tagged attributes as criteria for suggested next-step opportunities.

 

  • Recommendations are based on proximity of role in career path, associated attributes, roles in same job family, popularity of role within team, and open positions (if SF RCM is enabled).
  • Employees get clearer guidance on realistic career options.

Why it matters:
This drives employee ownership of development while aligning individual aspirations with organizational needs—creating a healthier, more sustainable succession pipeline.  From the employee perspective, they can see other roles in the organization where their skills and competencies are aligned and possibly create a new career aspiration they were unaware of.

 

  1. Enable Career Explorer

Career Explorer enables employees and leaders to visualize potential career paths across the organization and recommends opportunities based on the career paths of similar employees. This feature replaces the Suggested Roles functionality in the Career Worksheet, so it is recommended to enable one or the other—not both.

 

  • Shows adjacent, stretch, and future roles.
  • Highlights skill similarities and gaps between roles.

Why it matters:
Career Explorer supports internal mobility and future talent pipelines. Succession planners can use it to reveal non-obvious successor pools that may have transferable skills but sit outside traditional career paths.

Note:  There are specific prerequisites that are required to enable career explorer, such as Employee Central, Job Profile Builder enabled with mapped job classification codes, Succession enabled and at least 1,000 employee job change records, identified by job code changes. 

 

  1. Leverage the Opportunity Marketplace (additional license required for legacy licensing model but included in CTD new licensing)

The Opportunity Marketplace is a one stop shop for employees and managers to browse, create, enroll in and manage different types of opportunities.  Opportunities include internal projects, mentoring programs, temporary work projects and learning items. 

 

  • Employees can explore gigs, projects, and stretch opportunities to advance career options.
  • Each opportunity can be tied to skills and role requirements.

Why it matters:
Short-term opportunities accelerate skill development, making employees “succession-ready” faster. Employees who are engaged in their own development and seek out internal skill-building opportunities produces lower attrition numbers.  Leaders also gain real-time insight into who is building experience in critical skill areas.

 

  1. Drive Skill and Attribute Adoption Across the Workforce

The true power of TIH depends on participation. Create a campaign to introduce the Growth Portfolio and promote the ability to see the job architecture structure created for your organizations. 

  • Encourage managers and employees to work together to fill out and assess their skills and attributes in the Growth Portfolio.
  • Reinforce this during performance, development, and career conversations.

Why it matters:
The more complete the data:

  • The richer your succession plans become
  • The more accurate your workforce analytics are
  • The better your organization can forecast talent risks and opportunities

In short: better data = better decisions.

Final Thought: From System to Strategy

TIH is not just a system—it’s a strategy enabler. When fully enabled and widely adopted, it connects skills, roles, readiness, and opportunities in a single talent ecosystem.

By activating the features above and encouraging employee engagement, organizations can:

  • Build stronger succession pipelines
  • Identify future skills early
  • Improve internal mobility
  • Make workforce planning truly data-driven

TIH gives you the insight—how you use it determines the impact.

Implementing Talent Intelligence Hub (TIH) is a major milestone—but the real value lies in how effectively it is utilized. When fully enabled, TIH serves as a powerful engine for workforce planning, succession planning, and internal mobility, shifting organizations from reactive talent decisions to proactive, data-driven strategies.

entry.title: Identifying Leaders via Direct Reports in the Performance–Potential Matrix (Succession Planning)
entry.content:
Introduction

In one of our recent project, we had a business case where the client wanted to identify and filter employees who are leaders or managers within the Performance–Potential Matrix in Succession Planning. Additionally, they wanted the Succession icon to display only for those employees who hold a leadership role.

As per current system design in Succession Management, there is no standard field directly available for identifying leaders based on team size. To meet this requirement, we configured a simple yet robust solution using Integration Center, custom fields, standard field and matrix icon configuration.

To achieve this outcome, we used Integration Center to find whether an employee is a leader based on the number of direct reports. This logic was then associated to both a standard field (to control the Succession icon) and a custom field (to enable filtering within the Performance–Potential Matrix).

Below are the step to be followed:

1. Select any unused standard field—in our case, we used businessSegment—and rename it to “Is Leader?”. Define a picklist with the values Yes/No to this field.

2. Choose an available custom field, name it “Is Leader?”, and define the same Yes/No picklist.

3. In Integration Center, configure if/then/else conditional logic using the teamMemberSize field from the User entity for both the standard and custom field.

  • If teamMemberSize ≥ 1, populate the field with the optionId for Yes.
  • Otherwise, populate it with the optionId for No.

(Ensure the correct picklist option IDs for Yes/No are used.)

 


 

 

 

 

4. Validate both that data is populating correctly in the standard and custom fields using Test View.

5. Once the values are populating as expected, configure Scheduling so that the data continues to update regularly in both fields.

6. Download the latest Succession Data Model and update the attribute matrix-filter="yes" on the respective field. This enables business users to filter leaders/managers directly in the Performance–Potential Matrix.

7. Configure succession icon for standard field 'businessSegment'
 
8.  As a result, the custom field is now successfully added as a matrix filter in the Performance–Potential Matrix. Choose 'generate' to get output.
Conclusion: 
This solution provides a way to identify leaders using existing system capabilities. By combining Integration Center logic with custom and standard fields, organizations can enhance Succession Planning insights without requiring complex development or extensions.

Thank you for reading this article!

Feel free to share your thoughts, questions, or alternate approaches.

entry.title: Re: 启用批次和分割评估后，无法使用3+S
entry.content:
Hello @PANY 

Thank you for your question.

The answer provided is based on the following Machine translation of your question: 

"Batch processing has been enabled alongside split evaluation, with the evaluation category set to X. During material master creation, the 2+V option was selected. Upon receipt of the production order, the system automatically generated an evaluation type where the evaluation type code equalled the batch code. However, when viewing the material's ‘Accounting View 1’ via the evaluation type, the price control changed to 2+S. What is the reason for this? Our actual requirement is for 3+S".

 

Below is my response

In the scenario for single batch valuation with valuation category X, SAP creates a valuation header record for the material and dependent valuation records for each batch. The dependent valuation type is automatically created, and its code is normally the same as the batch code.

The important rule you should know here is that for the valuation header record, only price control V is allowed. For the dependent valuation records at valuation type level, the system (SAP) allows either V or S. Because of that, it is normal that after production order receipt, the automatically created valuation type shows S, even though the header material was created with 2+V.

Regarding the combination 2+S versus 3+S, SAP states that:

  • With price determination 2, both S and V are possible
  • With price determination 3, only S is possible.

So, the reason you are seeing 2+S is that the system created the batch specific valuation record with standard price but it did not switch the price determination control from 2 to 3 automatically. If your aim is 3+S, that needs to be supported by the overall valuation design and costing setup. It is not something that is automatically derived just because the valuation type was created from a batch during goods receipt.

See the following reference for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/d5699934e7004d048c4801b552f3b013/9c62e3f7092744d49021051301263e85.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/7f47a6d9441c46ac86c96fd27f6015f0/6cf1d353ca9f4408e10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Classification check table
entry.content:
In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, you cannot freely create check tables like in on-premise systems. To link a check table to a characteristic (CT04), the data element of the characteristic must exactly match the key field of the Custom Business Object (CBO). The error you’re seeing means the field types are not aligned — simply using “Text, length 30” is not sufficient. You must use a proper code list or value help defined in the CBO and ensure the characteristic references the same domain

Below are the steps how to create a valid check table in Public Edition

1. Use a Custom Business Object (CBO) with Correct Field Type
Define the primary key field in the CBO with the same data element/domain as the characteristic. For example, if your characteristic is CHAR(30), the CBO key must also be CHAR(30). If your characteristic is NUMC(10), the CBO key must be NUMC(10).

2. Publish the CBO as a Value Help
In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, check tables are implemented through Value Help definitions. Steps for that is to create the CBO in the Custom Business Objects app, Mark the key field as code list / value help enabled and publish the CBO so it becomes available as a value help.

3. Assign the Value Help to the Characteristic
Go to CT04 and create your characteristic. Under Additional Data → Value Assignment, select the published value help from your CBO. The system will only allow assignment if the data format matches exactly.

4. Common Pitfalls

Mismatch in field type: Even if both are “Text,” the internal data element may differ (e.g., CHAR vs. STRING).
Length mismatch: A characteristic with CHAR(30) cannot link to a CBO field defined as CHAR(20).
Wrong key definition: The CBO must have a single key field that matches the characteristic.

I would highly appreciate your help, since it is difficult to find good documentations on this. 

Thanks a lot, and best regards, 

Linda

entry.title: Re: Prepare for refresh with /SDF/CALM_SYS_REFRESH
entry.content:
Hello Werner,

you need to specify a target system for your ToC otherwise it will not create co and datafiles , like you already mentioned.

you can give the qas or prd system as target but do not import this transport in these systems (check if you have maybe import all job running in your target system).

After you released the transport just delete it from the buffer in the target system. you can then use the co and datafile to import it into your DEV System after systemrefresh.

otherwise you can also check sap note

entry.title: Re: Filter Value help results in Child entity based on pa...
entry.content:
@Shivani_Prabahar 

In OData v4 annotations, the localElement in @Common.ValueList parameters can only reference fields present in the same entity. Navigation paths to parent entities (e.g., to_Parent/CompanyCode) are not supported as filter sources.

One workaround is to expose the parent's field in the child entity so it becomes available as a localElement in the value help binding.

entry.title: Re: nJIT S2P with External Replenishment (Component Mater...
entry.content:
Try with the below debugging steps

  • Run transaction /JITMON (JIT Monitoring) and check the action log for the JIT call. It will show whether the inbound delivery creation action was attempted or skipped.
  • Check customizing in SPRO → Just-in-Time → Action Control to confirm the inbound delivery creation action is mapped to your scenario.
  • Use SMQ2/SMQ1 (qRFC queues) to see if there are stuck entries preventing the status update.
  • Ensure the inbound delivery number is correctly stored in table JITCALLHDR or related JIT tables.


Common missing Configurations

  • The action profile does not include inbound delivery creation.
  • The link between consignment order and JIT call is incomplete.
  • The update message from LE to JIT is not active (missing ALE/IDoc or SOAP callback).
  • The integration relevance is set incorrectly, so JIT doesn’t expect an inbound delivery.

In short, the inbound delivery is being created, but the feedback loop to JIT is not firing. Focus on the action control profile and the integration message back from LE/MM to JIT. That’s where the missing status update usually lies.

entry.title: Clean Core approach with the ASAPIO Clean Core Check
entry.content:
Introduction 
In this blog, I am going to show how to use the ASAPIO Integration Add-on Clean Core Check as a proactive safeguard to ensure Clean Core compliance – without manually researching released objects, SAP Notes, or successor APIs. 

The idea behind the Clean Core Check is simple: whenever a payload or outbound object is defined, the framework validates whether the used tables or objects are released for usage in SAP S/4HANA (especially in cloud-ready or clean core scenarios). If not, it automatically suggests alternative released CDS views. 

In short: instead of discovering during an upgrade that a table like VBAK is not released, you detect it during design time – and receive a proposal such as I_SALESDOCUMENT as successor. 

This approach requires the ASAPIO Integration Add-on and is particularly relevant in S/4HANA environments where unreleased objects must be avoided. 

 

Why Clean Core matters 

SAP’s Clean Core strategy aims to: 

  • Reduce modification footprint 
  • Prevent direct access to non-released database tables 
  • Ensure upgrade stability 
  • Enforce usage of released APIs and CDS views 

Many classical SAP tables (e.g. VBAK, VBKD, VEDA) are technically available but not released for direct usage in S/4HANA Cloud or Clean Core compliant developments. 

Using them in custom extraction logic or event payloads creates technical debt and upgrade risk. 

This is exactly where the ASAPIO Clean Core Check comes into play. 

 

Example: Payload with classic SD tables 

Let’s assume we defined a Sales Order payload in the ASAPIO Payload Designer (transaction /ASADEV/DESIGN). 

We add the following tables: 

  • VBAK – Sales Document Header 
  • VBPA – Partner Data 
  • VEDA – Contract Data 
  • VBKD – Business Data 

The payload is configured and released. 

Now, instead of discovering issues during a system conversion or ATC run, we execute the Clean Core Check directly in the Payload Designer. 

 

Running the Clean Core Check 

Inside the Payload Designer, simply click on the Clean Core Check button in the toolbar. 

The framework evaluates all tables and objects used in the payload definition. 

 

 

What happens here? 

  • The system detects that VBAK, VBKD, and VEDA are not released objects 
  • It checks SAP’s released API model 
  • It proposes I_SALESDOCUMENT as an alternative CDS View 
  • It flags objects not listed in released content (e.g. VBPA) 

This happens instantly. 

 

Enabling the Clean Core Check  

Before the Clean Core Check can be executed, the corresponding repository configuration must be maintained. 

The check relies on the ASAPIO Clean Core repository, which contains the metadata about released and non-released SAP objects as well as successor CDS views. 

To activate and configure the check properly, follow the official documentation: 

https://asapio.com/docs/designer/?asl_highlight=clean&p_asid=1#use_clean_core_checks 

 

Refactoring to released CDS views 

After the Clean Core Check result, the next step is straightforward: 

  1. Remove the unreleased tables from the payload 
  1. Replace them with the suggested CDS view 
  1. Re-map the required fields 

In our example, instead of selecting from VBAK directly, we use CDS View I_SALESDOCUMENT. 

Benefits: 

  • Released API contract 
  • Semantic layer abstraction 
  • Upgrade stability 
  • Cloud readiness 
  • Compliance with Clean Core guidelines 

The payload can now be executed without violating SAP’s release strategy. 

 

Summary 

The ASAPIO Clean Core Check provides: 

  • Immediate detection of non-released tables 
  • Automatic suggestion of released CDS alternatives 
  • Support for SAP Clean Core strategy 
  • Reduced upgrade risks 
  • Faster development cycles 

Instead of reacting to ATC findings during system conversion, developers can proactively design compliant payloads from the beginning. 

It shifts Clean Core validation directly into the design phase. 

In other words: 

Configuration-driven integration, now also Clean Core compliant. 

Let me know if you would like to see a deeper technical walkthrough or how the check is implemented internally in the Add-on.

entry.title: DG Exceptions in ECC
entry.content:
Hi,

My question is almost two-fold. Firstly, we are using ECC currently and have a product that has DG exceptions under certain packaging. Currently our specification has all packaging types assigned under the Material Assignments, with one DG inheritance that applies to the specification as a Dangerous Goods across the board for the determined regulations. The packages with the exceptions have a material assignment on a separate DG_CL_SUB with the nuances. However, when customers are receiving SDSs via SDS Distribution, section 14 is not showing correctly. Is this because material assignments are assigned in multiple places? How can DG exceptions be managed?

 

Furthermore, how do we manage this moving forwards in S/4 HANA and also in the not-so-distance future of PC New?
